channel.title: Posts on evocations
channel.description: Recent content in Posts on evocations

item.description: Dear Reader, So the year’s comin’ to an end. Thought I’d look back and check in on what all I’ve been up to this year. It’s a long time, a year. That’s 365 days. A single day seems small in the scheme of things, but they sure do add up. For the first part of the year, I rid myself of the concept of “dates”, and set all my calendars to just display the day of the year it was.

item.title: Multipliers
item.description: Life is finite. How do you do the most you possibly can while you’re alive? Not in an inhuman, mechanical sense – doing as much as possible in pursuit of as deeply intricate a life as possible. In order to pursue more aims, faster, with less resistance, towards whatever you desire. As human beings, putting away hours every day, you can take two approaches. The first is additive. Every day, put away the hours.

item.title: Two destinies
item.description: I’ve always known what my Achilles heel is. Over the last few years I’ve grown distant from people. I’ve avoided making introductions, reaching out to strangers, etc. I’ve had my justifications: “I’m working on something,” “I don’t want to compromise myself to try to make an impression on them,” and similar thoughts come to mind. The true reason was always twofold. Elitism | I believed that no one could offer me anything new, and they were just wasting my time.

item.title: Curriculum
item.description: Computer education is DIY. Classes teach basics, point out the most popular methods, frameworks. The learning happens inbetween them. I’ve held a love-hate relationship with this reality for the four years I’ve spent programming. On the one hand, information is free, knowledge is out there, and you can teach yourself anything these days. On the other hand, it’s discouraging. My best resources are archaic books packed to the gills with information that you can’t effectively search through, along with Google, the mother of my despair, which you can effectively search through, but is so packed with garbage that it boggles the mind.

item.title: Movement in 'The Legend of Zelda'
item.description: In The Legend of Zelda for the NES, I noticed a cool trick used to keep people from getting stuck on the terrain. It looks like this: There’s something weird going on here that is hard to put your finger on, but impossible to miss as you’re playing the game. There’s an effect, some nuance of the movement, that keeps you from getting stuck on the terrain and keeps your sword in line with the enemies.

item.title: A game's role
item.description: My experience with game design has been a tenuous one. This is a summary of the development of my opinions on games over time: Age 9 – 16 | Love games. Sometimes get bored. Age 17 – Today | General distaste for games. Belief that some games are gold. Games meant a lot to me once, in the sort of child-like way that only can be felt once, then remembered fondly.

item.title: Output Fear
item.description: I recently read Steven Pressfield’s The War of Art. It was a great book, packed with great insight, hard-earned wisdom, and some interesting metaphysical thoughts. Ideas that really struck me: Creativity is holy | Pressfield insists on treating the magic of creation with the religious reverance it has seemingly lost to many. Turning pro | Amateur artists submit to resistance. If you really cared, you would do it full-time, no matter the consequences.

item.title: Modeling light with computers
item.description: Over the last few weeks, I’ve been poking around with a basic ray-tracer. You can find my code here. It’s been fun to tweak values, but it’s my intention to fully understand everything that’s going on under the hood. I tend to find that teaching a subject leads to deeper understanding. In pursuit of understanding, I’m going to explain the concepts involved at a basic level. Consider this to be a 30,000 foot overview of ray-tracing.

item.title: Introduction
item.description: Hi there, I’m Alex. This is my first post. The web has been so wonderful to me in my life (though I spend little time here now). I love old internet homepages. here are a couple of examples. There’s something so cute about how raw these pages are, and the way that personal things are shared so effortlessly, without fear. They seem to have had some confidence that we all forgot.
